steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Researchers want to evaluate different conservation strategies for langurs, but they cannot accurately count the langurs — meaning they cannot determine whether the population is increasing, decreasing, or stable. The inference must follow directly from this limitation.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — risk making inaccurate conclusions about the effectiveness of different conservation strategies.: If population change can't be reliably measured, there's no reliable way to tell whether a strategy is working — this follows directly from the counting problem. ✓
  • Choice B — cause other conservationists to adopt a new methodology for counting populations.: Recommending alternative methods goes beyond what the passage supports; the passage describes the problem, not a solution. ✗
  • Choice C — benefit from including species beyond the white-headed langur.: Introduces a generalization the passage never makes. ✗
  • Choice D — fail to consider less-well-known conservation approaches for the white-headed langur.: The passage doesn't suggest the team is ignoring any approaches — only that their counting method is unreliable. ✗
A is correct.
